commands.review_summary:
Adds systemd Result/NRestarts/StartLimitBurst parsing, detects start-limit exhaustion in gateway status and doctor hints, runs systemctl reset-failed before restart, and adds focused tests.

PR surface: Source +95, Tests +241. Total +336 across 8 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source/dependency level: current main collapses failed systemd runtime into generic stopped guidance and restart omits reset-failed, while systemd exposes the fields and reset path the PR uses. I did not run a live current-main OpenClaw/systemd reproduction in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Contributor or human follow-up is needed for real behavior proof; there is no narrow automated code repair to queue from this review.

target.title: fix(gateway): surface systemd start-limit exhaustion
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The managed gateway's systemd unit ships `Restart=always` with `StartLimitBurst=5` /
`StartLimitIntervalSec=60`. When the gateway crashes faster than that allows, systemd latches the
unit into `failed` and **stops auto-restarting it entirely**. OpenClaw collapses that terminal state
into a generic `status: "stopped"`, so `openclaw gateway status` and `openclaw doctor` only report
*"Service is loaded but not running (likely exited immediately)."* — there is no signal that systemd
has given up, and nothing ever clears the failed latch, so on systemd versions that refuse a start in
that state the gateway cannot be recovered with `openclaw gateway restart`.

## Why This Change Was Made

A crash-looped, given-up service is a distinct, actionable state (the systemd analogue of a
Kubernetes CrashLoopBackOff), not an ordinary stop. Surfacing it and making the existing restart
command recover it turns a silent dead end into a one-command fix, using state systemd already exposes.

The change is two small parts:

1. **Detect the give-up.** `readSystemdServiceRuntime` now also reads `Result`, `NRestarts`, and
   `StartLimitBurst`. Detection is `state === "failed" && (Result === "start-limit-hit" ||
   (StartLimitBurst > 0 && NRestarts >= StartLimitBurst))`. The counter path is the important one:
   once the gateway process has actually run and exited non-zero, systemd keeps `Result = exit-code`
   and never overwrites it with `start-limit-hit`, so `Result` alone misses real crash loops. While a
   unit is still inside its restart cycle its state is `activating`, not `failed`, so the check only
   fires at the terminal give-up.
2. **Recover on restart.** `openclaw gateway restart` now runs `systemctl reset-failed <unit>` before
   `restart`. It is idempotent (a no-op on a healthy unit) and clears the failed/start-limit latch so a
   crash-looped gateway comes back up.

Presentation changes are confined to the existing `stopped` branch of `openclaw gateway status` and
`openclaw doctor`; all other wording, health computation, the generated unit, and non-systemd backends
are untouched.

## User Impact

- A crash-looped gateway now reports an actionable line — *"systemd stopped restarting the gateway
  after repeated crashes; run `openclaw gateway restart` or inspect logs."* — instead of the
  misleading "exited immediately."
- `openclaw gateway restart` reliably recovers a unit that systemd has stopped restarting.
- No new config, env var, flag, or unit change. An ordinary single failure still shows the existing
  "likely exited immediately" hint.

## Evidence

- **Unit tests** (`pnpm test`): `service-runtime.test.ts` + `doctor-format.test.ts` (12), `systemd.test.ts`
  (92, incl. parse fixtures, runtime classification, and `reset-failed`-before-`restart` ordering across
  user/sudo/machine-fallback/system scopes), `status.print.test.ts` (18). `oxfmt --check` clean;
  `tsgo` core + test-types green.
- **Real systemd 249 capture.** A throwaway crashing user unit (`ExecStart=/bin/false`,
  `StartLimitBurst=2`, `Restart=always`) was driven past its start limit:
  - Give-up state (read with the new property set): `ActiveState=failed`, `SubState=failed`,
    `Result=exit-code`, `NRestarts=2`, `StartLimitBurst=2` — confirming `Result` stays `exit-code`
    (not `start-limit-hit`) and that `NRestarts == StartLimitBurst` marks the give-up.
  - `systemctl --user reset-failed` returned the unit to `Result=success`, `NRestarts=0`, `inactive`,
    confirming the recovery path.
